comments.author: @Beagle5ce
comments.text:
DeepSeek的模型不一定是最强的, 但DS承担的是探路者的角色. 只要这条路能走通, 后续其他国产模型厂商就可以用更低的成本直接走这条通路而不需要再去试错走一遍.
比如Kimi本身就是基于DS的架构做的预训练和后训练, GLM更是直接使用了DSA这样的DS注意力机制. 让这些模型厂商可以集中财力和精力攻克应用层面的技术难题.

结果已经看到了, K2.6和GLM5.1已经能和闭源模型掰掰手腕了. 那么在DSv4诞生后, 配合昇腾950的部署. 其它国产模型同样会在DS铺好的路上受益. 训练出参数量更大,性能更强的模型.

comments.text: So, you discussed my comment (AI actually misspelled my username by the way )...I believe I watched  the whole original video where my comment was posted, and the explanation that you provided I don't think was there (could be wrong of course, I don't memorize your videos nor whether I watched the entire thing)...anyways, in this video you go over what seems to be an X post, which I obviously didn't see, otherwise I would not have commented the way I did...that said, your response in this video is basically assuming that making self driving trivial is going to "take a long time"...well, how do you know? of course you don't know, nobody knows...and that IS the risk, that some scrappy/resourceful person or team, develops a highly efficient way to teaching AI how to drive VERY SOON, and then Tesla's software advantage is gone...as far as your following rationale regarding what matters beyond software (e.g. cost, network effects etc), these are partially valid...meaning, the way Tesla could get screwed is that if the software issue was resolved as per my previous argument, then that person/team would license the software to vehicle manufacturers (likely Chinese, as they are the only ones what could realistically scale this quickly enough to trouble Tesla), and compete with Tesla (again, likely only in markets where Chinese vehicles are able to be sold, which would likely not include US, but would likely include everywhere else).  I would bet that if the software problem was solved, the Chinese would have cars running it within 3-6 months at full scale, saturating the market (which we all know, saturating the current taxi/uber market would not require that many cars (and yes, the market would expand, but would be constrained by 2D roads for potentially a long time).  Is it likely that Tesla will lose the software advantage soon? comments.author: @irongiant7577
comments.text:
Saying Tesla has already "won" self-driving ignores the rest of the world because of confirmation bias. Tesla’s big advantage is mostly stuck in North America. In other major markets, their FSD software is locked out by local laws. You can't just ignore China, the biggest EV market on earth. Companies there like Baidu, Huawei, and Xpeng already have legal, highly advanced self-driving cars and robotaxis on public roads right now. Tesla doesn't have a global monopoly; they are just leading in a Western bubble while massive competitors have built out their own systems overseas.

The video also completely misunderstands how AI training actually works. Thinking that whoever has the most driving data automatically gets the best system is just wrong. Having billions of miles of boring, straight-line highway driving doesn't teach a car how to handle weird, rare situations, like a crazy pedestrian or a confusing construction zone. Competitors skip this brute-force method by using highly detailed simulations to focus purely on those tough, rare scenarios. In AI, figuring out the hardest 1% of driving is way more valuable than hoarding billions of miles of easy, repetitive data.

This argument also ignores the physical limits of Tesla's camera-only setup. Tesla is betting everything on just using cameras to save money on manufacturing. Almost every other successful self-driving company uses a mix of sensors, including radar and LiDAR. Cameras force the car's computer to guess depth and distance from flat pixels, which can fail dangerously from bad sun glare, heavy rain, or weird lighting. LiDAR, on the other hand, gets an exact 3D map of the real world. The rest of the industry knows you need that physical backup to actually be safe in unpredictable weather.

Finally, bragging about having millions of cars on the road mixes up glorified cruise control with actual self-driving. Tesla’s massive fleet is mostly made of Level 2 systems, meaning the human driver is still legally on the hook if the car crashes. Yes, Tesla recently started running small, restricted robotaxi fleets in Texas, but competitors like Waymo and Chinese operators have been running huge, totally driverless networks for years, and those companies actually take the legal responsibility for the driving. Until a car maker is willing to take full legal and financial blame for every car they sell, calling this a victory is just jumping the gun.
